subjective: Onset & course: insidious vs. stepwise (vascular) vs. rapid
Cognitive domains affected: memory (short > long-term), language (word-finding), visuospatial, executive function
BPSD: agitation, wandering, hallucinations, depression, sleep disturbance
Functional decline: ADL (dressing, bathing, feeding) vs. IADL (finances, driving, medications)
Caregiver burden / social support
Medications (anticholinergics, sedatives), vascular risk factors, family history
objective: Level of consciousness, orientation × 4 (person, place, time, situation)
Language: spontaneous speech, naming (confrontation), repetition, comprehension
Memory: immediate recall, 5-min delayed recall, registration
Visuospatial: clock drawing, copy of interlocking pentagons
Executive: digit span (forward/backward), verbal fluency (F-A-S / animal naming)
Cranial nerves: visual fields, fundoscopy (papilledema?), extraocular movements
Motor: tone, drift, gait (broad-based, shuffling, apractic), Parkinsonism signs
Primitive reflexes: grasp, snout, palmomental (frontal release)
CDR (Clinical Dementia Rating) global score: 0 / 0.5 / 1 / 2 / 3
assessment_pearls: Alzheimer's (G30): amnestic > other domains; insidious onset
Vascular (F01): stepwise deterioration; focal signs; vascular risk factors
Lewy Body (G31.83): fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, Parkinsonism, REM sleep disorder
FTD (G31.09): behavioral/language > memory; young onset; disinhibition or apathy
MCI (G31.84): subjective + objective decline, intact ADL
plan_template: Cognitive: consider ChEI (donepezil/rivastigmine for AD/DLB) or memantine (moderate-severe)
BPSD: non-pharmacologic first; low-dose risperidone/quetiapine if necessary (avoid in DLB)
Vascular risk: control HTN, DM, dyslipidemia, antiplatelet/anticoagulation as indicated
Safety: driving assessment, fall prevention, wandering alert
Caregiver support: dementia day care, respite, long-term care planning
Follow-up imaging/labs: CT/MRI, CBC, TSH, B12, VDRL, HIV, metabolic panel

bedside_cognitive: MoCA (0–30): MCI <26; dementia <18; adjust +1 for <12 yrs education
neurologic_exam: Orientation: person, place, time (year, month, date, day), situation
Frontal release signs: grasp reflex, palmomental reflex, snout reflex, glabellar tap
Gait: initiation hesitation, shuffling, apraxia, turning en bloc
Parkinsonism: resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia
Visual: confrontation fields, fundoscopy
Extrapyramidal: facial masking, postural instability (pull test)

subjective: Seizure semiology: focal (aware vs. impaired awareness vs. bilateral tonic-clonic) or generalized
Aura: déjà vu, rising epigastric sensation, visual/auditory phenomena, focal onset clue
Duration, frequency, clusters; time from last seizure
Postictal state: confusion duration, Todd's paralysis, headache
Triggers: sleep deprivation, alcohol/withdrawal, fever, menstrual cycle, missed medications
First seizure vs. established epilepsy; prior workup (MRI, EEG, genetics)
Current AED regimen, adherence, side effects, serum levels
objective: Interictal exam is usually normal; check for focal deficits suggesting structural etiology
Mental status: baseline cognition, medication effects
Skin: café-au-lait spots, ash-leaf macules (TSC), port-wine stain (Sturge-Weber)
Head: macrocephaly/microcephaly
Cranial nerves: visual fields (occipital lesion?), papilledema
Motor: tone, reflexes, Babinski (focal cortical lesion)
Gait, coordination (medication effect vs. structural)
assessment_pearls: ILAE 2017 classification: focal / generalized / combined / unknown
Etiology: structural, genetic, infectious, metabolic, immune, unknown
Status epilepticus: >5 min continuous OR ≥2 seizures without return to baseline
SUDEP risk: nocturnal seizures, poor control, generalized tonic-clonic, young male, isolated living
Driving restriction: varies by jurisdiction (typically 6–12 months seizure-free)
plan_template: AED selection: focal → lamotrigine/levetiracetam/oxcarbazepine; GTC → valproate/lamotrigine
Women of childbearing age: avoid valproate; folate supplementation
Monitor: serum AED levels, CBC, LFT, electrolytes per drug
EEG: interictal, prolonged, or video-EEG monitoring
MRI brain (3T epilepsy protocol) if new or poorly controlled
Seizure diary; lifestyle counseling (sleep, alcohol, swimming supervision)
Surgical evaluation if drug-resistant (≥2 appropriate AEDs)

bedside_scales: Seizure frequency diary / calendar
Liverpool Seizure Severity Scale
QOLIE-89 / QOLIE-31 (quality of life in epilepsy)
neurologic_exam: Interictal neuro exam: cranial nerves, motor (drift, tone, reflexes), Babinski
Cognitive screening: MMSE/MoCA (AED cognitive effects)
Skin inspection: neurocutaneous stigmata (TSC ash-leaf, NF café-au-lait, Sturge-Weber)
EEG findings: background frequency, epileptiform discharges (spike/sharp wave/spike-wave), focal slowing
Ictal signs if witnessed: automatisms, version (head/eye turning = contralateral), tonic-clonic, postictal paralysis (Todd's)

subjective: Tremor: onset (dominant hand first?), rest vs. action/postural vs. intention; pill-rolling
Bradykinesia: reduced arm swing, micrographia, hypophonia, hypomimia, slow gait
Rigidity: stiffness, difficulty rising from chair, freezing of gait
Falls: frequency, injury, near-syncope vs. postural instability
Non-motor PD: constipation, anosmia, REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams), autonomic (OH, drooling, seborrhea)
Essential tremor: family history, alcohol responsiveness, bilateral action tremor
Dystonia: sustained posturing, task-specific (writer's cramp), cranial (blepharospasm/torticollis)
Medication history: antipsychotics, metoclopramide, valproate (drug-induced), SSRI
objective: Tremor characterization: frequency (~4–6 Hz PD; ~8–12 Hz ET), amplitude, distribution
Bradykinesia: finger tapping speed/amplitude (R vs. L), rapid alternating hand movements, foot tapping
Rigidity: cogwheel (PD) vs. lead-pipe; Froment's maneuver to augment
Postural instability: pull test (retropulsion ≥2 steps = positive)
Gait: stride length, arm swing asymmetry, festination, turning (en bloc vs. normal)
Speech: volume (hypophonia), rate (tachyphemia), articulation
Facial: hypomimia (masked facies), blink rate
Orthostatic BP: supine → standing at 1 and 3 min (OH: ≥20/10 mmHg drop)
Cognitive: brief MoCA/MMSE (PD-MCI or PDD)
Dystonia: abnormal posturing of neck/trunk/limbs, gestes antagonistes
assessment_pearls: PD (G20): asymmetric rest tremor + bradykinesia + rigidity; good levodopa response
PD with OH (G20.A1): add midodrine/fludrocortisone; fall risk high
MSA: early OH, cerebellar signs, poor levodopa response, urinary incontinence
PSP: early falls backward, vertical gaze palsy, axial > appendicular rigidity
DLB: cognitive + Parkinsonism + visual hallucinations (overlap with dementia category)
Essential Tremor (G25.0): bilateral action/postural tremor, family history, alcohol responsive
Drug-induced Parkinsonism (G21.19): symmetric, bilateral, no tremor at rest; identify offending drug
plan_template: PD: start levodopa/carbidopa (or pramipexole if young) when functional impairment
PD neuroprotection: exercise (≥2.5 hrs/wk), multidisciplinary PT/OT/speech
ET: propranolol 40–320 mg/d or primidone 50–250 mg/d
Dystonia: botulinum toxin injection (focal dystonia every 12 weeks)
DBS referral: motor fluctuations/dyskinesias despite optimal medication
Fall prevention: PT, home safety, walking aid
Autonomic (OH): salt/fluid loading, compression stockings, midodrine, fludrocortisone

bedside_scales: MDS-UPDRS Part III (motor subscale, 0–132): rate tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, gait, posture
Hoehn & Yahr stage (1–5): 1=unilateral, 2=bilateral, 3=postural instability, 4=significant disability, 5=wheelchair
Timed Up and Go (TUG): >12 s = fall risk
Spiral drawing (Archimedes spiral): amplitude tremor quantification
Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) for PD-MCI
neurologic_exam: Resting tremor: observe hands at rest in lap; 4–6 Hz pill-rolling
Action tremor: arms outstretched (postural); finger-nose-finger (intention = cerebellar)
Rigidity: passive elbow/wrist flexion-extension; Froment's maneuver (contralateral hand tapping)
Bradykinesia: finger tapping (R vs. L separately), hand pronation-supination, foot tapping (15 s count)
Facial: blink rate (normal ~20/min; PD ~5/min), smile symmetry
Gait: initiation, stride length, arm swing, turning (count steps), festination
Pull test: stand behind patient, firm tug backward; ≥2 steps back = positive (postural instability)
Orthostatic BP measurement protocol: supine 5 min → stand immediately → 1 min → 3 min

subjective: Time of onset (last known well) — critical for tPA/EVT eligibility window
FAST: Facial droop, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call
Deficits: hemiparesis (which side?), facial droop, dysarthria, aphasia, hemianopia, diplopia, dysphagia, ataxia, vertigo
Headache severity (thunderclap → SAH), neck stiffness (meningeal)
TIA: fully resolved symptoms? duration? multiple episodes?
Vascular risk factors: HTN, DM, AF, hyperlipidemia, smoking, prior stroke/TIA, CAD
Medications: anticoagulants, antiplatelets, antihypertensives, OCPs
Stroke mimics: migraine, Todd's paralysis, hypoglycemia, conversion disorder
objective: NIHSS (0–42): document score at admission and follow-up
BP (both arms if subclavian steal suspected), HR (AF?), O2 sat
Level of consciousness: alert, drowsy, stupor, coma (1a, 1b, 1c in NIHSS)
Gaze: conjugate deviation (toward lesion = frontal; away = pontine/thalamic), gaze palsy
Visual fields: confrontation test, hemianopia (MCA/PCA territory)
Facial: upper vs. lower motor neuron pattern (forehead sparing = UMN = cortical/capsular)
Motor: pronator drift, arm/leg weakness grade (0–5 MRC), side of deficit (contralateral to lesion)
Sensory: hemisensory loss, neglect (double simultaneous stimulation)
Language: fluency (non-fluent = Broca/frontal; fluent + comprehension deficit = Wernicke/temporal)
Coordination: dysmetria, ataxia (ipsilateral = cerebellar)
Gait: hemiparetic scissor gait vs. ataxic vs. small-stepped
assessment_pearls: Anterior circulation (MCA, ACA, ICA): contralateral hemiparesis, face > arm > leg, aphasia (dominant), neglect (non-dominant)
Posterior circulation (PCA, BA, PICA): crossed deficits, diplopia, vertigo, ataxia, Horner's, dysphagia
Lacunar: pure motor, pure sensory, ataxic hemiparesis, dysarthria-clumsy hand (deep white matter/BG/pons)
ICH (I61): headache at onset, early neurological deterioration, elevated BP, characteristic CT pattern
SAH (I60): thunderclap headache ('worst of life'), stiff neck, photophobia, sentinel bleed
TOAST classification: large artery, cardioembolic, small vessel, other, undetermined
plan_template: Acute: tPA within 4.5 h (if no contraindications); EVT for large vessel occlusion within 24 h
Secondary prevention: antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel × 21 d for minor stroke/TIA); anticoagulation for AF (NOAC preferred)
Risk factor modification: BP <130/80, LDL <70 mg/dL (high-risk), HbA1c, smoking cessation
Cardiac monitoring: prolonged telemetry, echocardiogram, Holter/ECG loop recorder (cryptogenic)
Neuro-rehabilitation: PT (mobility), OT (ADL, upper limb), speech (aphasia/dysphagia), swallowing assessment (FEES/MBS)
Post-stroke depression screening: PHQ-9; dysphagia screen before oral intake; DVT prophylaxis
Follow-up imaging: MRI DWI, MRA/CTA, carotid Doppler

bedside_scales: NIHSS (0–42): 0=normal, 1–4=minor, 5–15=moderate, 16–20=moderate-severe, 21–42=severe
mRS (0–6): 0=no symptoms, 1=no significant disability, 2=slight disability, 3=moderate disability, 4=moderately severe, 5=severe disability, 6=death
Barthel Index (0–100): activities of daily living
ASPECTS (0–10): early ischemic changes on CT (EVT selection; <6 poor outcome)
ABCD2 score (TIA risk stratification): 0–7; ≥4 = high risk of early stroke
neurologic_exam: Level of consciousness: alertness, orientation (time/place/person)
Gaze: conjugate deviation side, gaze palsy, gaze-evoked nystagmus
Visual fields: finger confrontation (each quadrant, each eye)
Facial asymmetry: show teeth, raise eyebrows (UMN vs. LMN pattern)
Motor arms: pronator drift (hold outstretched 10 s), MRC 0–5 each limb
Motor legs: leg drift (hold at 30° × 5 s), dorsiflexion strength
Sensory: pin-prick, light touch, proprioception; double simultaneous (neglect test)
Language: name objects, repeat 'No ifs, ands, or buts', follow 3-step command
Dysarthria: repeat 'mama/papa/baby' — slurring vs. clear
Cerebellar: finger-nose-finger, heel-shin, rapid alternating movements, Romberg

subjective: Mechanism of injury: direct blow, acceleration-deceleration, blast, fall height
Loss of consciousness (LOC): duration (none / <30 min = mild; 30 min–24 h = moderate; >24 h = severe)
Post-traumatic amnesia (PTA): duration from injury to continuous memory formation
Headache: onset, location, severity (VAS), character
Post-concussion symptoms: dizziness/vertigo, cognitive fog (difficulty concentrating), memory problems, fatigue, sleep disturbance, emotional lability, photophobia, phonophobia, visual disturbance
Prior TBI history ('second impact syndrome' risk)
Current medications: anticoagulants (hemorrhage risk), NSAIDs (analgesic)
objective: GCS (3–15): E4V5M6 = normal; ≤8 = severe TBI
Vital signs: BP/HR (Cushing's triad = elevated BP + bradycardia + irregular respiration = herniation)
Pupil exam: size, reactivity, asymmetry (>1 mm = herniation concern)
Scalp: lacerations, haematoma, skull step-off

Cranial nerves: anosmia (CN I, frontal impact), diplopia (CN IV/VI), facial weakness (CN VII), hearing loss (CN VIII, temporal fracture), Battle's sign (mastoid bruising), raccoon eyes (anterior fossa fracture)
Motor: focal deficits (contralateral to mass lesion), decerebrate/decorticate posturing (severe)
Cognitive: orientation, attention, memory (3-word recall at 5 min), MACE score
Balance: BESS (Balance Error Scoring System), tandem walk, Romberg
Vestibular: VOMS (Vestibular/Ocular Motor Screening): smooth pursuits, saccades, horizontal VOR, VOR cancellation, visual motion sensitivity, near point of convergence
assessment_pearls: Mild TBI (concussion): GCS 13–15, LOC <30 min, PTA <24 h, no intracranial pathology on CT
Moderate TBI: GCS 9–12 OR LOC 30 min–24 h OR PTA 1–7 days
Severe TBI: GCS ≤8 OR LOC >24 h OR PTA >7 days
Return-to-play (sports): stepwise protocol (rest → light aerobic → sport-specific → non-contact → full contact → return); each step 24 h minimum
Post-concussion syndrome: symptoms >4 weeks (mild) or persistent; monitor for depression
Intracranial hemorrhage types: EDH (lucid interval, temporal bone fracture, MMA), SDH (elderly/alcohol, bridging veins), SAH (thunderclap), contusion (coup-contrecoup)
plan_template: Acute: CT head if GCS <15, LOC, amnesia, focal deficit, vomiting, anticoagulants, age >65, coagulopathy (Canadian CT Head Rule / NEXUS II)
Mild/concussion: relative cognitive and physical rest × 24–48 h; graded return to activity
Pain: acetaminophen preferred over NSAIDs/opioids (avoid masking deterioration); avoid aspirin (hemorrhage)
Post-concussion: vestibular PT, cognitive rehabilitation, sleep hygiene, screen for depression/anxiety
Moderate-severe: neurosurgical consultation, ICP monitoring (EVD if GCS ≤8), mannitol/HTS for ICP crisis, prevent secondary injury (MAP ≥80 mmHg, SpO2 ≥95%, temp ≤37.5°C, glucose 6–10 mmol/L)
Rehabilitation: early mobilization, cognitive PT, OT, speech, psychology, neuropsychology
Seizure prophylaxis: phenytoin/levetiracetam × 7 days for severe TBI (not proven to prevent late epilepsy)

bedside_scales: GCS (3–15): Eyes 1–4, Verbal 1–5, Motor 1–6; E+V+M; ≤8=severe
MACE (Military Acute Concussion Evaluation): orientation × 4, 5-word recall, digit span, balance
SCAT6 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool): symptom checklist (22 items), cognitive, balance
BESS (Balance Error Scoring System): 6 stances × 20 s; count errors
VOMS (Vestibular/Ocular Motor Screening): 7 tests, any score >2 = abnormal
King-Devick test: rapid number identification; slower than baseline = positive
Rivermead Post-Concussion Symptoms Questionnaire (RPQ)
neurologic_exam: GCS: open eyes (spontaneous/command/pain), verbal response, motor response
Pupils: size (mm), direct and consensual reactivity, asymmetry
Cranial nerves: anosmia (coffee/vanilla), visual acuity, EOM, facial sensation, hearing (finger rub)
Battle's sign: bruising over mastoid (temporal bone fracture)
Raccoon eyes / periorbital ecchymosis: anterior cranial fossa fracture
Motor and reflexes: pronator drift, plantar response, deep tendon reflexes
Coordination: finger-nose, tandem gait, Romberg with eyes closed
Near Point of Convergence (NPC): normal <5 cm; >10 cm post-concussion
Smooth pursuits: follow finger horizontally/vertically without saccades (note any catch-up saccades)

subjective: Location: unilateral (migraine, cluster) vs. bilateral (tension); vertex, occipital, hemicranial
Quality: throbbing/pulsating (migraine), pressure/band-like (tension), stabbing/electric (trigeminal autonomic, ice-pick), thunderclap (SAH, RCVS)
Severity: VAS 0–10; average vs. peak
Duration: episodic (4–72 h migraine; 15–180 min cluster; 30 min–7 d tension) vs. chronic (≥15 d/month)
Frequency: episodic (<15 d/mo) vs. chronic (≥15 d/mo)
Aura: visual (fortification spectra, scotoma, zigzag lines), sensory (unilateral paresthesias), motor (hemiplegic migraine), speech (aphasia)
Associated symptoms: nausea/vomiting, photophobia/phonophobia, osmophobia, lacrimation, nasal congestion, ptosis, conjunctival injection (autonomic = cluster/SUNCT)
Triggers: menstruation, sleep deprivation, stress, alcohol (red wine), skipped meals, bright lights, strong smells, weather change
Medication use frequency: analgesics ≥10 d/mo = medication overuse headache (MOH) risk
Red flags (SNOOP4): Systemic illness, Neurologic deficit, Onset sudden (thunderclap), Older age (>50 new), Positional (worse lying), Papilledema, Progressive, Pregnancy
objective: Vital signs: BP (hypertensive headache), fever (meningitis/encephalitis)
General: signs of systemic illness, BMI (IIH risk with obesity)
Neurologic exam: typically NORMAL in primary headache; any abnormality → secondary cause
Pericranial tenderness: temporal artery palpation (GCA if age >50, tenderness, elevated ESR), pericranial muscle tenderness (tension)
Fundoscopy: papilledema (ICP elevation, IIH)
Meningeal signs: nuchal rigidity, Kernig's, Brudzinski's (meningitis/SAH)
CN exam: ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis (Horner's in Raeder's/cluster); CN VI palsy (ICP), CN III palsy (PCA aneurysm)
ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria applied
assessment_pearls: Migraine (G43): ≥5 attacks; 4–72 h; ≥2 of (unilateral, pulsating, moderate-severe, worsened by activity); + nausea/vomiting OR photophobia+phonophobia
Migraine with aura (G43.1): aura ≤60 min, fully reversible, followed by/concurrent with headache
Tension-type (G44.2): bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not worsened by activity, no N/V, one of photophobia/phonophobia
Cluster (G44.0): unilateral orbital/supraorbital, 15–180 min, severe, + ipsilateral autonomic (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis), restlessness
Medication Overuse Headache (G44.40): chronic daily headache + analgesic/triptan overuse; treat by withdrawal
Secondary headache alarms: thunderclap, new >50 years, fever/stiff neck, papilledema, focal deficit, postural worsening, HIV/cancer
plan_template: Acute migraine: NSAIDs/acetaminophen (mild); triptan (moderate-severe, no CV contraindication); anti-emetic (metoclopramide, domperidone); IV ketorolac/valproate/DHE in ED
Acute cluster: 100% O2 × 15 min (non-rebreather mask); subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg
Migraine prevention (≥4 d/mo): topiramate, valproate, amitriptyline, propranolol, candesartan, CGRP mAb (erenumab/fremanezumab)
CGRP antagonist (gepant): acute – ubrogepant/rimegepant; preventive – atogepant
Tension-type prevention: amitriptyline, mirtazapine, stress management, sleep hygiene
MOH: bridge therapy + analgesic withdrawal; preventive medication
Referral: secondary causes ruled out, chronic/refractory, medication counseling

bedside_scales: VAS (0–10) or NRS pain scale
HIT-6 (Headache Impact Test): ≤49 little/no impact; 50–55 some; 56–59 substantial; ≥60 severe
MIDAS (Migraine Disability Assessment): grade I–IV
PHN-Q (headache diary: frequency, duration, triggers, medications)
neurologic_exam: Fundoscopy: disc margins (papilledema = raised ICP), spontaneous venous pulsation
Temporal artery: tenderness, thickening, reduced pulse (GCA in age >50)
Pericranial muscle tenderness: frontalis, temporalis, masseter, trapezius, SCM (tension-type)
Meningeal signs: neck stiffness (chin to chest), Kernig's (leg extension pain with hip flexed 90°), Brudzinski's (involuntary leg flexion when neck flexed)
ICHD-3 diagnostic checklist application
Cranial nerves: pupil asymmetry (Horner's in cluster, III palsy in aneurysm), visual fields
Trigger points: occipital nerve (greater/lesser occipital neuralgia), Tinel's sign at occipital notch

subjective: Trigeminal neuralgia (G50.0): paroxysmal electric-shock/stabbing facial pain; triggers (touching face, eating, talking, cold air); V2/V3 distribution; side of face
Bell's palsy (G51.0): acute unilateral facial weakness; onset within hours–days; ear pain, hyperacusis, taste loss, dry eye/mouth; viral prodrome
Hemifacial spasm (G51.3): unilateral involuntary facial twitching; starts around eye; triggered by fatigue/stress
Glossopharyngeal neuralgia (G52.1): throat/ear stabbing pain, triggered by swallowing
Cranial nerve palsy: binocular diplopia, ptosis (CN III), facial numbness, hearing loss (CN VIII), dysarthria, dysphagia (CN IX–XII)
objective: CN V (Trigeminal): facial sensation (V1/V2/V3 – forehead/cheek/jaw), corneal reflex (afferent V1, efferent VII), jaw deviation (opens away from weak side), masseter bulk
CN VII (Facial): forehead wrinkling (upper face = bilateral cortical supply), eye closure, smile symmetry, nasolabial fold; taste anterior 2/3 tongue; hyperacusis; House-Brackmann grade
CN III (Oculomotor): ptosis, eye position (down-out), pupil size (mydriasis = posterior communicating artery aneurysm, 'surgical' CN III palsy)
CN VI (Abducens): lateral gaze limitation, inward deviation (esotropia)
CN IV (Trochlear): vertical diplopia worsened looking down and in; head tilt opposite side
CN VIII (Vestibulocochlear): hearing (finger rub, Weber/Rinne tuning fork), Dix-Hallpike (BPPV), head impulse test (vestibular neuritis)
CN IX–X: gag reflex, uvula deviation, palate elevation, voice quality (nasal vs. hoarse)
CN XI: trapezius and SCM strength
CN XII: tongue protrusion (deviates to weak side), fasciculations, atrophy
assessment_pearls: TN (G50.0): classic = idiopathic (NVC); symptomatic = MS (young bilateral) or tumor; MRI with CISS/FIESTA sequence
Bell's palsy (G51.0): LMN pattern (all facial muscles including forehead); treat within 72 h with prednisolone; antivirals if severe; eye care critical
Central vs. peripheral facial palsy: central (UMN) spares forehead; peripheral (LMN) involves forehead
Ramsay Hunt (G53, B02.21): severe pain + vesicles in external auditory canal + facial palsy; treat with valacyclovir + steroid
Painful CN III palsy: pupil-involving = posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise — emergency CT/CTA
plan_template: TN: carbamazepine 200–1200 mg/d (first-line); oxcarbazepine; baclofen; MVD surgery or gamma knife for refractory
Bell's palsy: prednisolone 60 mg/d × 5 d, taper 10 d total (within 72 h of onset); valacyclovir 1 g TID × 7 d if severe; eye lubrication, taping at night
CN III palsy: emergent imaging (CTA/MRA) if pupil-involved; MRI for ischemic
Hemifacial spasm: botulinum toxin injection every 12 weeks; MVD if refractory
Vestibular neuritis: corticosteroids (methylprednisolone); vestibular rehabilitation PT

bedside_scales: House-Brackmann grade (Bell's palsy): I=normal, II=slight, III=moderate (eye closure with effort), IV=moderately severe (incomplete closure), V=severe, VI=total paralysis
Sunnybrook Facial Grading Scale
VAS for TN pain intensity
neurologic_exam: CN I (Olfactory): coffee/vanilla identification (anosmia after TBI or early PD)
CN II (Optic): visual acuity, color vision (red desaturation), visual fields confrontation, RAPD (swinging flashlight test), fundoscopy
CN III/IV/VI (Ocular motor): primary gaze position, full EOM in all 9 directions, cover test, convergence, pupil size and reactivity
CN V (Trigeminal): light touch cotton wool V1/V2/V3, pin-prick, corneal reflex, jaw clench power
CN VII (Facial): raise eyebrows, shut eyes tight (resistance), puff cheeks, show teeth, House-Brackmann grade
CN VIII: whisper test, Rinne (air > bone = normal), Weber (lateralizes to bad ear in CHL, good ear in SNHL), Dix-Hallpike, Head Impulse Test
CN IX-X: soft palate rise ('ahh'), gag reflex, voice quality
CN XI: shoulder shrug against resistance (trapezius), SCM strength (turn head against resistance)
CN XII: tongue protrusion midline, lateral movement speed, fasciculations

categories.nameZh: 周邊神經病變／神經壓迫

subjective: CTS (G56.01/02 right/left): numbness/tingling digits 1–3 ± 4 radial half; worse at night; relief by 'flicking' hand (Flick sign); weakness grasping/pinching
Ulnar neuropathy (G56.21/22 right/left): numbness digits 4–5; weakness intrinsics (grip, finger spread); 'claw hand' (ring/little fingers); ulnar elbow tenderness
Peroneal neuropathy (G57.31/32 right/left): foot drop, slapping gait; numbness dorsum of foot and lateral lower leg
Meralgia paresthetica (G57.11/12): lateral thigh numbness/burning; worse standing/walking; obesity/belt/pregnant
Radial neuropathy (G56.31/32 right/left): wrist drop, weakness finger/wrist extension; sensory loss dorsal hand/1st web space
Tarsal tunnel (G57.51/52): plantar foot burning/tingling; worse walking; positive Tinel's at medial malleolus
Polyneuropathy (G62): length-dependent, distal > proximal; stocking-glove pattern; DM/alcohol/chemotherapy/hereditary
Duration, precipitating factors (repetitive use, compression positions), occupation
objective: Sensory: pin-prick and light touch in specific nerve territory vs. dermatomal (root) vs. length-dependent (polyneuropathy)
Motor: specific muscle weakness in nerve territory — CTS (thenar APB); ulnar (FDI, ADM, hypothenar); peroneal (tibialis anterior, EHL, peroneus longus/brevis)
Reflexes: biceps (C5/musculocutaneous), brachioradialis (C6/radial), triceps (C7/radial), patellar (L4/femoral), Achilles (S1/tibial) — absent in polyneuropathy
Tinel's sign: percuss over nerve at compression site → radiating tingling (CTS at wrist; ulnar at cubital tunnel; tarsal tunnel at medial malleolus)
Phalen's test (CTS): wrist flexion 60 s → symptoms in digits 1–3
Carpal compression test: direct pressure over carpal tunnel 30 s
Elbow flexion test (ulnar): maximal elbow flexion + wrist extension 60 s → medial elbow/ring-little tingling
Straight leg raise (SLR): neural tension test for sciatic/peroneal (L4-S1)
Muscle bulk: thenar atrophy (CTS), hypothenar atrophy (ulnar), anterior tibial atrophy (peroneal)
assessment_pearls: CTS localization: at wrist (not elbow/shoulder); NCV: prolonged median sensory + motor distal latency at wrist
Ulnar: most common site = cubital tunnel (elbow) not Guyon's canal (wrist); NCV: conduction block/slowing across elbow
Peroneal at fibular head: NCV: conduction block/slowing; differentiate from L4-L5 root (SLR negative; no hip abductor weakness in peroneal)
Polyneuropathy (G62): NCV/EMG to classify axonal vs. demyelinating, length-dependent vs. non-length-dependent; evaluate for GBS if acute
GBS (G61.0): ascending weakness, areflexia, CSF protein-cell dissociation, autonomic instability; admit for monitoring
plan_template: CTS: wrist splinting (neutral) at night; corticosteroid injection; carpal tunnel release if severe/failed conservative
Ulnar: elbow pad, avoid pressure; cubital tunnel release or transposition if severe
Peroneal: address compression (fibular head padding, weight loss, positioning); AFO for foot drop; decompression if focal compression
Polyneuropathy: treat underlying cause (glucose control for DM, thiamine for alcohol, IVIG for CIDP/GBS)
Pain: pregabalin 75–300 mg BID, duloxetine 60–120 mg/d, TCAs (amitriptyline 10–75 mg); topical lidocaine/capsaicin
NCV/EMG: confirm diagnosis, localize lesion, severity, prognosis
PT: foot drop brace (AFO), strengthening, gait training

bedside_scales: Michigan Neuropathy Screening Instrument (MNSI): 10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament + vibration
NRS pain 0–10 for neuropathic pain
DN4 questionnaire (neuropathic pain: burning, electric shock, tingling, numbness, itching) ≥4/10 = neuropathic
neurologic_exam: 10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament: apply at 5 plantar sites; can't feel = loss of protective sensation
Vibration sense: 128 Hz tuning fork at great toe/lateral malleolus; compare proximal (tibia) if distal absent
Pin-prick/temperature: glove-stocking pattern (polyneuropathy) vs. dermatomal (radiculopathy) vs. nerve territory (mononeuropathy)
Tinel's sign: CTS (anterior wrist), cubital tunnel (medial elbow), tarsal tunnel (medial malleolus)
Phalen's test: both wrists maximally flexed 60 s; CTS symptoms in digits 1–3
Grip strength (Jamar dynamometer): compare R vs. L (dominant > 10% stronger is normal)
Two-point discrimination: fingertip (<5 mm normal), great toe
Muscle power: APB (thenar) for CTS, FDI (first dorsal interosseous) for ulnar, tibialis anterior for peroneal, EHL, gastrocnemius/soleus

categories.nameZh: 脫髓鞘疾病（多發性硬化症）

subjective: Relapse (attack): new or worsening neurologic symptom lasting >24 h, not due to fever/infection
Common presentations: optic neuritis (painful vision loss, worse with eye movement, RAPD), limb weakness, sensory disturbance, diplopia, cerebellar ataxia, bladder urgency/retention
Lhermitte's sign: electric sensation down spine on neck flexion (posterior column)
Uhthoff's phenomenon: symptom worsening with heat/exercise/fever
Bowel/bladder: urgency, frequency, retention, incontinence, constipation
Fatigue: multiple sclerosis fatigue (profound, not related to exertion), Modified Fatigue Impact Scale
Disease course: RRMS, SPMS, PPMS; time since diagnosis; prior relapses and recovery; current DMT
objective: EDSS (Expanded Disability Status Scale): 0–10; <3 = minimal disability; 6 = need walking aid; 8 = bedridden
Motor: pyramidal signs — hyperreflexia, Babinski, clonus, spasticity (Ashworth scale), weakness
Cerebellar: ataxia (heel-shin, finger-nose), intention tremor, dysarthria, nystagmus
Sensory: vibration loss (posterior columns), dermatomal vs. spinal cord level (Brown-Séquard)
Visual: visual acuity, color vision (red desaturation), RAPD (swinging flashlight), fundoscopy (optic disc pallor after optic neuritis)
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO): adduction deficit ipsilateral eye + nystagmus contralateral eye on lateral gaze
Bladder: post-void residual ultrasound
Cognitive: Brief International Cognitive Assessment for MS (BICAMS): SDMT, BVMT-R, CVLT
assessment_pearls: McDonald 2017 criteria: dissemination in space (DIS) + dissemination in time (DIT) from clinical + MRI + CSF
DIS: ≥2 of 4 CNS regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord)
DIT: new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, OR oligoclonal bands in CSF
NMOSD (AQP4-IgG, MOG-IgG): severe optic neuritis (bilateral), longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis ≥3 segments — distinguish from MS
CIS (G37.3 / G35 first attack): single demyelinating event; high conversion to MS if MRI lesions
plan_template: Acute relapse: methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3–5 days (speeds recovery, not long-term outcome)
DMT (RRMS): platform (IFN-β, glatiramer) vs. moderate (dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide) vs. high-efficacy (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab, cladribine)
PPMS: ocrelizumab (anti-CD20) approved
Symptom management: spasticity (baclofen, tizanidine), bladder (anticholinergics, ISC), fatigue (amantadine, modafinil), pain (gabapentin, duloxetine), depression (SSRI)
Monitoring: annual MRI, EDSS, 25-foot walk, 9-HPT, SDMT, ophthalmology (OCT)
Rehabilitation: PT, OT, speech, fatigue management, cognitive rehabilitation
Vaccination: avoid live vaccines on immunosuppressive DMT; annual influenza

bedside_scales: EDSS (0–10): functional systems (pyramidal, cerebellar, brainstem, sensory, bowel/bladder, visual, cerebral)
25-foot walk timed test (T25FW): >6 s = significant impairment
9-Hole Peg Test (9-HPT): upper limb dexterity
Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT): processing speed (cognitive)
Ashworth Scale (0–4): spasticity grade
Modified Fatigue Impact Scale (MFIS)
neurologic_exam: Visual: Snellen VA, color plates (Ishihara), RAPD swinging flashlight, fundoscopy (disc pallor)
INO test: ask patient to look left and right — adduction lag + abducting nystagmus (ipsilateral INO)
Pyramidal: upper/lower limb strength MRC 0–5, hyperreflexia, sustained clonus (>3 beats), Babinski
Cerebellar: finger-nose (intention tremor, past-pointing), heel-shin, rapid alternating movements, Romberg, gait ataxia
Sensory: vibration 128 Hz tuning fork great toe → tibia → sternum; pin-prick level; dermatomal or spinal level
Posterior column: vibration + proprioception loss with preserved pin-prick (Brown-Séquard = ipsilateral motor + vibration loss, contralateral pain/temp loss)
Bladder: ask about urgency/frequency/retention; consider post-void residual US

subjective: Location: cervical (neck pain ± arm radiation), thoracic (chest/mid-back), lumbar (LBP ± leg radiation/sciatica)
Character: aching (discogenic/facetogenic) vs. radicular (electric/shooting in dermatomal pattern) vs. myelopathic (spastic gait, hand clumsiness)

Radiculopathy red flags: which dermatome? Cervical C5 (deltoid), C6 (thumb), C7 (middle finger), C8 (little finger); Lumbar L4 (medial leg, quad), L5 (dorsum foot, EHL), S1 (lateral foot, plantar, Achilles)
Myelopathy symptoms: 'clumsy hands' (buttoning difficulty, dropping objects), 'heavy/stiff legs', imbalance/falls, bowel/bladder urgency
Aggravating/relieving: extension (stenosis), flexion (disc herniation), prolonged sitting, walking distance (neurogenic claudication vs. vascular)
Trauma history, osteoporosis risk (compression fracture), red flags (fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, cancer history, immunosuppression, IV drug use)
Prior treatments: PT, injections, surgery
objective: Posture: antalgic lean (away from herniation side), forward-stooped (flexion bias = stenosis), scoliosis
ROM: cervical (flexion/extension/lateral bending/rotation), lumbar (Schober test: normal >5 cm increase in 10 cm mark)
Palpation: midline tenderness (vertebral body fracture, infection), paraspinal tenderness (muscle spasm), SI joint
Neurologic: dermatomal sensory loss, myotomal weakness (key muscles), DTR changes (diminished in radiculopathy, hyperreflexic in myelopathy)
Cervical myelopathy signs: Hoffmann's sign, inverted radial reflex, Babinski, clonus, wide-based ataxic gait
Cervical radiculopathy: Spurling's test (compress + ipsilateral side bend/extension → reproduces arm pain), ULNT (upper limb neural tension)
Lumbar radiculopathy: SLR (L4-S1; positive if pain radiates below knee at <70°), crossed SLR (highly specific), Slump test, femoral nerve stretch (L2-L4, prone knee flexion)
SI joint: FABER test, posterior SI compression, Gaenslen's test
assessment_pearls: Radiculopathy vs. referred pain: radiculopathy = dermatomal, electric, reproduced by neural tension tests
Neurogenic claudication vs. vascular: NC = walking aggravates, bending forward (shopping cart sign) relieves; vascular = stops at fixed distance, quickly relieved, no postural component
Cervical myelopathy (M47.12): urgent MRI; Hoffmann's + Babinski + hyperreflexia + gait = surgery referral
Acute cauda equina: bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder retention/incontinence = surgical emergency
Yellow flags: psychosocial factors predicting chronicity (fear-avoidance, catastrophizing, poor job satisfaction)
plan_template: Acute LBP (<6 weeks): continue activity (avoid bed rest), NSAIDs/acetaminophen, muscle relaxant short-term, heat
Radiculopathy: NSAIDs, gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic component, short course oral steroids; epidural steroid injection if refractory
PT: McKenzie method (extension exercises for posterior disc), core stabilization, traction
Cervical myelopathy: surgical decompression (laminoplasty/ACDF/corpectomy); delay worsens prognosis
Injection therapy: medial branch block/RFA (facet), ESI (radiculopathy), SI joint injection, trigger point injection
Surgery indications: progressive neurologic deficit, cauda equina, failed conservative >6–12 weeks, instability
Red flag workup: CBC, ESR, CRP (infection/malignancy), CT/PET (cancer), MRI with gadolinium

bedside_scales: VAS/NRS pain 0–10
Oswestry Disability Index (ODI): 0–100%; >40% = severe disability
Neck Disability Index (NDI): 0–50 total; >35% = severe
mJOA (Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association): cervical myelopathy severity (0–18; <12 = surgical candidate)
neurologic_exam: Spurling's test: tilt head to symptomatic side + extend + compress axially; positive = reproduces arm/shoulder pain; sensitivity 50%, specificity 94%
SLR (Lasègue): supine, passive hip flexion with knee straight; positive = sciatica pain radiates below knee at 30–70° (L4-S1)
Crossed SLR: raising contralateral leg reproduces ipsilateral sciatica; highly specific for disc herniation
Slump test: seated slump + neck flexion + knee extension + dorsiflexion; positive = sciatic tension symptoms
Femoral nerve stretch (reverse SLR): prone, flex knee; positive = anterior thigh pain (L2–L4)
Hoffmann's sign: flick middle finger DIP; positive = flexion/adduction of thumb + index finger (upper cervical myelopathy)
Inverted radial reflex: tap brachioradialis; finger flexion instead of elbow flexion = C5/6 radiculopathy + myelopathy
Schober test: mark L5S1 and 10 cm above; forward flexion; normal = >15 cm (≥5 cm increase)
FABER test: hip Flexion, ABduction, External Rotation ('figure 4'); pain = SI joint or hip pathology
Kemp's test: extend + rotate lumbar spine toward pain; positive = facet or foraminal compression

subjective: Pain character: deep, aching, worse with weight-bearing and activity; 'startup pain' (first steps after sitting = OA), then eases; end-of-day worsening
Stiffness: morning stiffness <30 min (OA) vs. >60 min (RA); gelling (stiffness after inactivity that eases with movement)
Knee: medial > lateral joint line; stairs difficulty; giving way (varus deformity + medial OA); swelling/effusion
Hip: groin/anterior thigh pain; radiation to knee; decreased walking distance; difficulty getting out of chair/car
Hand: DIP/PIP joints (Heberden/Bouchard nodes); base of thumb (1st CMC = trapezio-metacarpal = 'CMC OA'); difficulty jar-opening, pinching
Prior injuries, activity level, BMI, occupation, family history
Medications: NSAIDs (GI/renal risk), analgesics, prior injections (corticosteroid vs. hyaluronic acid vs. PRP)
objective: Gait: antalgic gait (short stance phase on painful side), Trendelenburg lurch (hip abductor weakness)
Knee: alignment (varus = medial OA most common, valgus = lateral OA), effusion (bulge sign, patellar tap), crepitus (audible/palpable), joint line tenderness (medial > lateral), ROM (normal 0–135°)
Hip: ROM (flexion, IR, ER — IR limited first in OA), FABER (pain in groin = hip joint), FADIR (hip pathology), Patrick test
Hand: Heberden nodes (DIP OA), Bouchard nodes (PIP OA), 1st CMC grind test (pain + crepitus at thumb base = CMC OA), pinch strength
Valgus/Varus stress tests (knee): medial (MCL) and lateral (LCL) stability
McMurray test (knee): knee flexion + medial/lateral rotation → click/pain = meniscal tear (common comorbidity)
Muscle strength: quadriceps (knee) — often weak in OA; hip abductors (hip OA)
assessment_pearls: Radiographic Kellgren-Lawrence grade 0–4: 2–3 = moderate OA (joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis)
Bilateral primary OA hip (M16.0), unilateral R (M16.11), unilateral L (M16.12)
Unilateral primary OA knee R (M17.11), L (M17.12), bilateral (M17.0)
Secondary OA: post-traumatic, post-inflammatory, post-surgical
Differentiate from RA (symmetric, MCP/PIP, morning stiffness >1 h, RF/anti-CCP positive, erosions on X-ray)
plan_template: Non-pharmacologic (first-line): exercise (aerobic + strengthening), weight loss (5–10% body weight significantly reduces knee pain), PT, assistive devices (cane contralateral), knee brace (unloader for compartmental OA)
Topical: topical diclofenac gel (knee/hand); good tolerability
Oral analgesics: acetaminophen 1 g TID (mild-moderate), NSAIDs with gastroprotection (PPI), duloxetine (for central sensitization component)
Intra-articular: corticosteroid (acute flare, effusion); hyaluronic acid (3–5 injections, evidence moderate); PRP (emerging evidence)
Surgery: total knee/hip arthroplasty for severe OA with failed conservative; unicompartmental knee replacement for isolated medial OA
Hand OA: thumb splint (1st CMC), topical diclofenac, CMC steroid injection; trapeziectomy for severe CMC OA

bedside_scales: WOMAC (Western Ontario and McMaster Universities OA Index): 24 items, pain/stiffness/function subscales
KOOS (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score)
VAS/NRS pain
Timed Up and Go (TUG): >12 s = fall risk / functional limitation
neurologic_exam: Knee effusion: bulge sign (milk medial side, tap lateral → medial bulge = small effusion); patellar tap (large effusion); ballottement
Valgus stress test (LCL): apply valgus force at 30° flexion; laxity = LCL tear
Varus stress test (MCL): apply varus force at 30° flexion; laxity = MCL tear
McMurray test: flex knee fully → externally rotate (medial meniscus) + extend; snap/pain = positive
FABER / Patrick test: hip Flex-ABduct-Ext rotate ('figure 4'); pain at groin = hip OA, at SI = sacroiliitis
FADIR test: hip Flex-ADduct-Int rotate; pain/groin = intra-articular hip (OA, labral tear, FAI)
Grind test (1st CMC): axial load + rotation at thumb base; pain+crepitus = CMC OA
Heberden nodes (DIP) and Bouchard nodes (PIP): palpate for bony enlargement

subjective: Rotator cuff (M75.1): anterior/lateral shoulder pain; worse with overhead/reaching; night pain (unable to sleep on affected side); weakness with lifting/reaching
Frozen shoulder/adhesive capsulitis (M75.0): insidious global loss of ROM in all planes; night pain; phases: freezing (pain dominant) → frozen (stiffness dominant) → thawing
Impingement (M75.5): pain with arm elevation 60–120° 'painful arc'; overhead occupation
Biceps tendinopathy (M75.2): anterior shoulder pain; Speed's test; pain with resisted supination
Lateral epicondylitis/Tennis elbow (M77.1): lateral elbow pain with grip/wrist extension activities; worse with typing, racket sports; no trauma required
Medial epicondylitis/Golfer's elbow (M77.0): medial elbow pain with wrist flexion/forearm pronation
Dominant arm vs. non-dominant; occupation (repetitive overhead, vibration tools); prior injections/surgery
objective: Inspection: muscle atrophy (infraspinatus → posterolateral scapula wasting = chronic RC tear), shoulder asymmetry, scapular winging
Active ROM: forward flexion (normal 180°), abduction (180°), internal/external rotation
Passive ROM: differentiated from active; capsular pattern (IR > ER restriction = frozen shoulder)
Neer impingement sign: forward flex arm fully (passively); pain = positive (supraspinatus impingement against acromion)
Hawkins-Kennedy: 90° forward flex + internal rotation; pain = supraspinatus impingement in subacromial space
Empty Can (Jobe) test: arm 90° abduction, 30° forward flexion, IR (thumbs down); resist downward force; weakness/pain = supraspinatus
Drop Arm test: abduct arm to 90°; patient slowly lowers; sudden drop = full-thickness RC tear
Lift-off test (Gerber): dorsum of hand on small of back; resist lifting arm off back; weakness = subscapularis
Speed's test: shoulder 90° flex, elbow extended, forearm supinated; resist downward force; pain = biceps tendinopathy
Yergason's test: elbow 90° flex; resist supination/external rotation; pain in bicipital groove = biceps tendinopathy
Cozen's test (lateral epicondylitis): forearm pronated, wrist extend against resistance; pain over lateral epicondyle
Mill's test: elbow fully extend + forearm pronate + wrist flex; lateral elbow pain = lateral epicondylitis
Valgus stress (UCL): elbow 30° flex; apply valgus force; laxity/pain medially = UCL injury
assessment_pearls: Right (M75.121) vs. Left (M75.122) complete RC tear; partial tear (M75.111 R / M75.112 L)
Right (M75.01) vs. Left (M75.02) adhesive capsulitis
Right (M77.11) vs. Left (M77.12) lateral epicondylitis; Right (M77.01) vs. Left (M77.02) medial epicondylitis
AC joint OA: focal tenderness over AC joint; cross-body adduction test; active compression test (O'Brien)
Acute full-thickness RC tear: consider MRI; large tear (>3 cm) or younger patient → surgical repair
Frozen shoulder: rule out infection, DM-associated; 12–18 month natural course; capsular distension injection or manipulation under anesthesia for persistent cases
plan_template: Rotator cuff: PT (rotator cuff strengthening, scapular stabilization, posterior capsule stretching); NSAIDs; subacromial corticosteroid injection (short-term relief); consider PRP; surgical repair for full-thickness tears with functional deficit

Frozen shoulder: pain-dominant phase: analgesia + supervised neglect/gentle ROM; PT + suprascapular nerve block or intra-articular steroid; hydrodistension/capsular distension; manipulation under anesthesia; arthroscopic capsular release
Impingement: subacromial injection + PT; acromion morphology (type III) → acromioplasty if refractory

Lateral epicondylitis: activity modification, eccentric wrist extension exercises, counterforce brace (lateral epicondyle band), NSAID topical; injection (corticosteroid or PRP); surgery (Nirschl procedure) for refractory >6 months
Medial epicondylitis: similar to lateral; avoid valgus stress; PT; injection medial epicondyle

bedside_scales: Constant-Murley Score (0–100): shoulder function
DASH (Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand): 0–100, higher = more disability
ASES (American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons): 0–100
VAS/NRS pain
PRTEE (Patient-Rated Tennis Elbow Evaluation): pain + function
neurologic_exam: Painful arc: active abduction through range; pain from 60–120° = impingement; pain at end-range = AC joint
Empty Can (Jobe): 90° abd + 30° forward flex + IR thumbs-down; resist down → weakness/pain = supraspinatus
Full Can test: same as Empty Can but ER (thumbs up); weakness = supraspinatus
Drop Arm: passively abduct to 90°; patient slowly lowers — sudden drop = full RC tear
Neer's sign: passive forward flexion to 180° with internal rotation; pain = impingement
Hawkins-Kennedy: 90° forward flex + IR; pain = impingement
Lift-off (Gerber): dorsum of hand on lumbar spine; lift off against resistance → weakness = subscapularis
Belly-press test: press palm against abdomen; elbow wings forward to maintain force (if subscapularis weak)
Cozen's: wrist extension against resistance with elbow extended → lateral epicondyle pain
Mill's: elbow fully extended + forearm pronated + wrist passively flexed → lateral epicondyle stretch pain
Finkelstein's test: thumb folded into fist, ulnar deviate wrist → pain at radial styloid = De Quervain's tenosynovitis (M65.4)

categories.nameZh: 軟組織疾病／附著點病變

subjective: Plantar fasciitis (M72.2): heel pain, maximal at first steps in morning ('first step pain'); improves with walking but worsens with prolonged standing; tender medial calcaneal tubercle; Achilles tightness
Myofascial pain syndrome (M79.1): deep, aching muscle pain; reproducible trigger points; referred pain pattern (e.g., upper trapezius → ipsilateral temporal headache); stiffness
Fibromyalgia (M79.3): widespread bilateral pain >3 months; fatigue, non-restorative sleep, cognitive fog ('fibro fog'); allodynia, hyperalgesia; associated with anxiety/depression
Bursitis: localized swelling/tenderness over bursa (olecranon, prepatellar, trochanteric, pes anserine, retrocalcaneal)
Tendinopathy (non-specific): insertion pain with activity; no acute trauma; gradual onset; morning stiffness
Occupation, sports, BMI (plantar fasciitis risk), footwear (heel height, arch support)
objective: Plantar fasciitis: maximal tenderness medial calcaneal tubercle; pain reproduced by dorsiflexion of toes (Windlass test); limited ankle dorsiflexion (<10° = tight gastrocnemius-soleus); Silfverskjöld test (differentiate tight gastrocnemius vs. soleus)
Trigger points: taut band palpation, referred pain pattern on palpation, local twitch response (LTR), jump sign (involuntary withdrawal)
Fibromyalgia: ACR 2010/2011 criteria — widespread pain index (WPI ≥7) + symptom severity scale (SSS ≥5); tender points (historical, 18 points); normal joint exam
Trochanteric bursitis: lateral hip tenderness over greater trochanter; FABER (provokes), Ober test (ITB tightness)
Pes anserine bursitis: medial knee pain 3–4 cm below joint line; obesity/DM/OA associated
Olecranon bursitis: posterior elbow swelling; fluctuant if acute; hard if chronic/calcific
assessment_pearls: Plantar fasciitis (M72.2): clinical diagnosis; heel spur on X-ray is incidental (60% asymptomatic); ultrasound shows fascia thickening >4 mm
Myofascial pain (M79.1): distinct from fibromyalgia (regional vs. widespread); taut bands with referred pain; responds to dry needling/injection
Fibromyalgia (M79.3): central sensitization; exclude inflammatory/endocrine/neuro conditions; VAS 0–10 for widespread pain
Trochanteric pain syndrome (M79.3 / bursitis M70.6-): greater trochanteric pain; check for ITB tightness, hip abductor weakness; injection-guided if refractory
plan_template: Plantar fasciitis: stretching program (plantar fascia + Achilles); orthotics/heel cups; night splint; NSAIDs/topical; corticosteroid injection (short-term, limit to 2–3 to avoid fat pad atrophy/rupture); ESWT (extracorporeal shockwave) for chronic (>6 months); PRP injection
Myofascial pain: trigger point dry needling or injection (lidocaine/saline); PT (spray-and-stretch, deep tissue massage, TENS); address ergonomics and posture
Fibromyalgia: aerobic exercise (evidence grade A); duloxetine 60–120 mg/d, pregabalin 150–450 mg/d, milnacipran; sleep hygiene; CBT; avoid opioids
Bursitis: activity modification, compression; aspiration if large/tense (exclude infection); corticosteroid injection; surgical bursa removal if chronic/infected
ITB syndrome (M70.3-): stretching, foam rolling, hip abductor strengthening; corticosteroid injection

bedside_scales: VAS/NRS pain 0–10
ACR 2010 Fibromyalgia Criteria: WPI (0–19) + SSS (0–12); diagnosis if WPI ≥7 + SSS ≥5 OR WPI 4–6 + SSS ≥9
FAAM (Foot and Ankle Ability Measure) for plantar fasciitis
VISA-A (Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment — Achilles) for Achilles tendinopathy
neurologic_exam: Windlass test (plantar fasciitis): passive dorsiflexion of great toe with foot in plantar grade; reproduces heel pain = positive; sensitivity 32%, specificity 100%
Silfverskjöld test: compare ankle dorsiflexion ROM with knee extended vs. flexed; if limited with extended but improved when knee flexed = tight gastrocnemius (not soleus)
Trigger point palpation: perpendicular palpation of muscle belly; taut band with referred pain + local twitch response
Ober test: lateral decubitus, bottom hip flexed, top leg abducted and extended; lower leg: positive if hip does not adduct to table (ITB/TFL tightness)
Ely's test: prone, passively flex knee; hip rises off table = tight rectus femoris
Noble compression test (ITB): lateral knee compression at 30° flexion = ITB friction syndrome
Heel thump test: axial percussion along heel bone; pain = calcaneal stress fracture vs. plantar fasciitis
Tinel's sign at tarsal tunnel (medial malleolus): tingling into sole = tarsal tunnel syndrome

subjective: Onset & quality: spinning (true vertigo) vs. lightheadedness vs. unsteadiness
Duration: seconds (BPPV) vs. minutes (TIA/Meniere's episode) vs. hours (Meniere's) vs. days (neuritis)
Triggers: head position change (BPPV), loud noise/Valsalva (SCD), standing up (orthostatic)
Associated symptoms: nausea/vomiting, tinnitus, hearing loss, ear fullness (Meniere's)
Neurologic symptoms: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, facial numbness, limb weakness (central causes)
Fall history, prior episodes, head trauma, recent viral illness (neuritis)
Medications: aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, antiepileptics (ototoxic)
objective: Vital signs: orthostatic BP drop (>20/10 mmHg) for orthostatic hypotension
Nystagmus: direction (horizontal, vertical, torsional), fixation suppression (peripheral vs central)
HINTS exam: Head Impulse Test (abnormal = peripheral), Nystagmus (direction-changing = central), Test of Skew (skew deviation = central)
Dix-Hallpike test: positive with latent upbeat-torsional nystagmus = posterior canal BPPV
Romberg / Fukuda stepping test: peripheral — falls toward lesion side
Hearing: Weber and Rinne tests (sensorineural vs conductive loss)
Cerebellar exam: finger-nose, gait, tandem walk
assessment_pearls: BPPV (H81.1): most common; Dix-Hallpike positive; treat with Epley maneuver
Meniere's disease (H81.0): recurrent attacks of vertigo + unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + ear fullness
Vestibular neuritis (H81.2): acute, prolonged vertigo; HINTS normal HIT; no hearing loss
Labyrinthitis (H83.0): vestibular neuritis + hearing loss
BPPV vs central: HINTS exam — normal HIT + direction-changing nystagmus = central alarm
Posterior fossa stroke (PICA): sudden vertigo + HINTS — normal HIT is danger sign
Superior canal dehiscence (H83.8): Tullio phenomenon (sound-induced vertigo)
plan_template: BPPV: Epley maneuver (posterior canal); Barbecue roll (horizontal canal); reassess in 1 week
Acute vestibular neuritis: vestibular suppressants (short-term) + early vestibular rehab
Meniere's: low-sodium diet (<2g/day), diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide), betahistine
Central vertigo: urgent MRI brain (DWI), neurology/neurosurgery consultation
Vestibular rehabilitation: canalith repositioning, balance training for chronic unilateral vestibular loss
Avoid prolonged vestibular suppressants (delay central compensation)

bedside_scales: HINTS exam: Head Impulse + Nystagmus + Test of Skew — 100% sensitivity for stroke (if all normal = central)
Dix-Hallpike: position patient supine, head 45° to side; positive = latent upbeat-torsional nystagmus
Supine Roll (Barbecue): for horizontal canal BPPV; geotropic vs apogeotropic
Orthostatic BP: standing 1 min and 3 min after supine; drop >20/10 = orthostatic hypotension
DHI (Dizziness Handicap Inventory): 0–100; mild <30, moderate 30–60, severe >60
neurologic_exam: Spontaneous nystagmus: direction, inhibited by fixation (peripheral) or not (central)
Head impulse test (VOR): catch-up saccade = peripheral (intact HINTS = central alarm)
Skew deviation: vertical misalignment of eyes (central sign)
Gait: wide-based, tendency to fall ipsilateral to lesion (peripheral) vs. ataxic (central)
Cranial nerves: V, VII (facial), VIII (hearing), IX/X (dysphagia), XII (tongue)
Cerebellar signs: dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, truncal ataxia

subjective: Onset & progression: acute (<4 wks), subacute (4–8 wks), chronic (>8 wks); symmetric vs. asymmetric
Distribution: distal (length-dependent) vs. proximal (myopathy/NMJ) vs. focal (mononeuropathy/radiculopathy)
Sensory symptoms: numbness, tingling, burning, loss of proprioception (falls in dark)
Motor symptoms: weakness — difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs (proximal); hand dexterity (distal)
Autonomic symptoms: orthostatic hypotension, hyperhidrosis/anhidrosis, bladder dysfunction
Fatigability: worsening with repetitive use (NMJ) vs. constant weakness (myopathy)
Systemic context: diabetes, alcohol, chemotherapy, family history of neuropathy or muscular dystrophy
Medications: statins (myopathy), isoniazid (B6 deficiency neuropathy), amiodarone
objective: Muscle tone: hypotonia (LMN/myopathy) vs. spasticity (UMN)
Muscle bulk: wasting (chronic denervation/myopathy), hypertrophy (e.g., DMD calves)
Strength testing (MRC 0–5): proximal (deltoid, iliopsoas) vs. distal (grip, EDB, tibialis anterior)
Reflexes: diminished or absent (peripheral neuropathy/myopathy) vs. hyperreflexia (UMN overlap)
Sensory: stocking-glove loss, large-fiber (vibration, proprioception) vs. small-fiber (pin, temp)
Tinel sign: carpal tunnel, cubital tunnel; nerve percussion tenderness
Fatigability test: sustained upgaze ptosis (MG); repetitive grip decrement
Gait: steppage gait (foot drop), Trendelenburg (proximal hip weakness), waddling (myopathy)
assessment_pearls: Hereditary neuropathy (G60.0 Charcot-Marie-Tooth): AD/AR; distal wasting; high arches; pes cavus
Inflammatory neuropathy: GBS (G61.0) — ascending, areflexia, albuminocytologic dissociation; CIDP (G61.81) — chronic
Diabetic polyneuropathy (E11.42): length-dependent, small + large fiber; screen annually
Myasthenia gravis (G70.00/01): fatigable ptosis/diplopia/dysarthria; AChR or MuSK Ab; edrophonium test
Lambert-Eaton (G73.1): proximal weakness improves with repetition; P/Q VGCC Ab; lung cancer association
Inflammatory myopathy (G72.49/M33): proximal weakness; elevated CK; EMG myopathic; skin rash (DM)
GBS: treat with IVIG or plasmapheresis; monitor FVC (ventilatory failure risk)
plan_template: NCS/EMG: confirm axonal vs. demyelinating; pattern of involvement
Labs: CBC, CMP, HbA1c, TSH, B12, folate, SPEP (paraprotein), ANA/anti-dsDNA, AChR Ab, anti-MuSK, CPK
GBS: IVIG 2 g/kg or plasmapheresis; ICU monitoring; DVT prophylaxis
CIDP: IVIG (maintenance), corticosteroids, plasma exchange
MG: pyridostigmine (symptom control); immunosuppression (prednisone ± azathioprine); thymectomy if thymoma
Diabetic neuropathy: optimize glycemic control; pain management (gabapentin/duloxetine/amitriptyline)
Physiotherapy/OT: foot drop AFO, adaptive equipment, fall prevention

bedside_scales: MRC Scale (0–5): 0=no contraction, 1=flicker, 2=no gravity, 3=against gravity, 4=reduced resistance, 5=normal
ONLS (Overall Neuropathy Limitations Scale): arm 0–5 + leg 0–7
Quantitative sensory testing: 10g monofilament, vibration with 128 Hz tuning fork, pinprick
Myasthenia Gravis Foundation Classification (I–V): based on bulbar and respiratory involvement
Single-fiber EMG: most sensitive test for NMJ disorders (MG, LEMS)
Ice pack test: ptosis improves with ice (MG) — bedside confirmation
neurologic_exam: Cranial nerve exam: ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia (MG/LEMS/myopathy)
Fatigue test: sustained upgaze 1 min for ptosis; repeated arm raises for fatigable proximal weakness
Gowers' sign: patient uses hands to climb up legs when rising from floor (proximal hip/quad weakness)
Calf hypertrophy: pseudohypertrophy in DMD (fatty replacement of muscle)
Deep tendon reflexes: ankle/knee jerks absent in polyneuropathy; relatively preserved in myopathy
Sensory dermatomal vs. stocking-glove distribution
Horner syndrome: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis (pancoast tumor, lateral medullary syndrome)

subjective: Chief complaint: insomnia vs. hypersomnia vs. abnormal movements/behaviors during sleep
Sleep schedule: bedtime, wake time, total sleep time, sleep latency, awakenings
Insomnia: difficulty initiating vs. maintaining vs. early-morning awakening
Daytime impairment: Epworth Sleepiness Scale; driving safety
Snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping/choking (OSA); nocturia
Restless legs: urge to move legs, worse at rest, evening/night, relieved by movement
Parasomnias: sleep-walking, sleep terrors, REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams)
Circadian disruption: shift work, jet lag, delayed sleep phase
Medications and substances: caffeine, alcohol, hypnotics, stimulants, antidepressants
objective: BMI, neck circumference (>40 cm female / >43 cm male = OSA risk), Mallampati score
Oropharynx: tonsillar hypertrophy, retrognathia, macroglossia
Cardiovascular: hypertension (OSA association)
Neurologic: cognitive exam, evidence of narcolepsy (cataplexy trigger), RBD features
Akathisia vs. RLS: distinguish from medication-induced restlessness
assessment_pearls: OSA (G47.33): AHI ≥5 with symptoms or ≥15 regardless; STOP-BANG ≥3 = high risk
Narcolepsy type 1 (G47.411): EDS + cataplexy; CSF hypocretin low; MSLT: SOREMP ≥2
REM sleep behavior disorder (G47.52): violent dream enactment; 80% eventually develop synucleinopathy
Restless legs syndrome (G25.81): URGE criteria — Urge to move, Rest aggravates, Gets better with movement, Evening/night worsens
Insomnia disorder (G47.00): CBT-I is first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, relaxation)
Delayed sleep phase (G47.21): circadian misalignment; treat with light therapy + melatonin
Central sleep apnea (G47.31): Cheyne-Stokes in HF; treat underlying cause ± ASV
plan_template: OSA: CPAP titration; dental appliance for mild-moderate; positional therapy; weight loss; ENT referral for surgery
Insomnia: CBT-I first-line; short-term: zolpidem/eszopiclone or low-dose doxepin; sleep hygiene education
RLS: ferritin >75 mcg/L target; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); pregabalin; avoid augmentation
Narcolepsy: modafinil/armodafinil (EDS); scheduled naps; sodium oxybate (cataplexy + EDS)
RBD: clonazepam or melatonin; bedroom safety (padded floor, remove sharp objects); neurodegeneration counseling
Polysomnography: indicated for OSA, parasomnias, RBD, narcolepsy workup; MSLT for hypersomnia

bedside_scales: Epworth Sleepiness Scale (0–24): >10 = excessive daytime sleepiness
STOP-BANG questionnaire (0–8): score ≥3 = high OSA risk; ≥5 = very high risk
Insomnia Severity Index (ISI, 0–28): ≥15 = moderate-severe insomnia
PSQI (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index): score >5 indicates poor sleep quality
RLS severity scale (0–40): mild 1–10, moderate 11–20, severe 21–30, very severe 31–40
Mallampati score (I–IV): I = full uvula visible; IV = only hard palate
neurologic_exam: Cataplexy trigger: tell a joke or ask for funny story; observe for brief muscle weakness/collapse
Hypnagogic hallucinations: ask about vivid imagery at sleep onset
Sleep paralysis: unable to move at sleep onset/offset
RBD: ask bed partner about vocalization, punching, kicking during sleep
Akathisia vs RLS: RLS is position-dependent (supine/sitting at rest), worse at night, relieved by walking

subjective: Fever onset: acute (<24h) vs. subacute (days) vs. chronic (weeks)
Headache: severity, location, sudden vs. gradual onset
Meningismus: neck stiffness, photophobia, phonophobia
Altered consciousness: confusion, drowsiness, GCS level
Focal neurologic deficits: weakness, speech, vision, coordination
Seizures: type, duration, frequency
Skin rash: petechiae/purpura (meningococcal), vesicles (HSV)
Immune status: HIV, immunosuppressants, organ transplant
Exposure: travel, tick bites, TB contact, animal contact
Vaccination history: meningococcal, pneumococcal
Prior neurosurgery, CSF shunt, otitis/sinusitis, mastoiditis
objective: Vital signs: fever, BP, HR (Cushing triad if raised ICP)
GCS: eye, verbal, motor score
Meningeal signs: Kernig's sign (pain/resistance on knee extension with hip flexed 90°)
Brudzinski's sign: involuntary hip/knee flexion on neck flexion
Jolt accentuation of headache (2–3 Hz horizontal rotation = positive)
Fundoscopy: papilledema (raised ICP, contraindication to LP)
Cranial nerves: CNIII palsy (herniation), CNVI palsy (raised ICP)
Motor: focal weakness, pyramidal signs, Babinski
Skin: petechiae, purpura, vesicles, herpes labialis, rash distribution
ENT: otitis media, mastoid tenderness, sinus tenderness
assessment_pearls: Bacterial meningitis emergency: empiric antibiotics BEFORE LP if CT needed or LP delayed
Common pathogens: S. pneumoniae (adults), N. meningitidis (young adults), L. monocytogenes (elderly/immunosuppressed)
Viral encephalitis: HSV-1 most common treatable cause — empiric acyclovir started empirically
LP contraindications: coagulopathy, papilledema, focal deficits, altered consciousness → CT first
CSF bacterial: WBC>1000 neutrophils, low glucose (<2/3 serum), high protein, + culture/Gram stain
CSF viral: WBC 10-500 lymphocytes, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein
Cryptococcal meningitis: AIDS patients, India ink, cryptococcal antigen (serum + CSF)
TB meningitis: subacute, lymphocytic pleocytosis, very low glucose, high protein, AFB PCR
Autoimmune encephalitis: anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR — rule out if no infectious cause
plan_template: Empiric bacterial meningitis: ceftriaxone 2g q12h + vancomycin 15–20mg/kg q8-12h IV
Add dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg q6h × 4 days (especially S. pneumoniae)
Add ampicillin 2g q4h if immunosuppressed, >50 years, or pregnant (L. monocytogenes)
HSV encephalitis: acyclovir 10mg/kg q8h IV × 14–21 days (MRI temporal lobe + EEG)
Cryptococcal: amphotericin B 0.7–1mg/kg/day + flucytosine (induction 2 weeks) → fluconazole
TB meningitis: RIPE + dexamethasone; total 9–12 months treatment
Raised ICP: HOB 30°, osmotherapy (mannitol 20% 0.5–1g/kg or hypertonic saline), avoid hypotension/hypoxia
Seizure management: levetiracetam IV if seizures occur
Contact prophylaxis (N. meningitidis): ciprofloxacin 500mg single dose for household/close contacts
Notify public health: N. meningitidis, H. influenzae type b are notifiable diseases

bedside_scales: GCS (3–15): <8 = comatose; consider intubation
FOUR Score (0–16): eye, motor, brainstem, respiration — better for intubated patients
NIHSS for focal deficits if relevant
Modified Rankin Scale (mRS) at discharge for outcome
neurologic_exam: Meningeal signs: neck stiffness (degrees of flexion possible), Kernig's, Brudzinski's, Jolt accentuation
Cranial nerves: CNII (visual acuity/fields, fundoscopy), CNIII/IV/VI (eye movements, ptosis, pupil), CNVII (facial symmetry)
Motor: focal weakness — MRC grade 0-5, pyramidal distribution, bilateral vs unilateral
Reflexes: hyperreflexia (encephalitis), absent reflexes (severe sepsis, GBS)
Sensory: dermatomal deficits, vibration, proprioception
Cerebellar signs: ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria (brainstem/cerebellar encephalitis)
Brainstem reflexes: corneal, gag, oculocephalic, vestibulo-ocular (herniation assessment)
Focal or generalized seizure observation/documentation

subjective: CP type: spastic (hemiplegia/diplegia/quadriplegia), dyskinetic (choreoathetoid), ataxic, mixed
GMFCS level I–V (Gross Motor Function Classification System)
Ambulatory status: independent, with aids (walker/crutches), non-ambulatory
Upper extremity function: MACS level I–V
Communication: verbal, AAC devices, speech intelligibility level
Feeding/swallowing: oral intake, G-tube, aspiration history, nutritional status
Seizure history: type, frequency, current AED regimen
Pain: spasticity pain, hip dislocation pain, GI pain, back pain
Orthopedic history: hip surveillance results, scoliosis degree, contractures, prior surgery
Spasticity management: BoNT-A injections (last date, muscles injected), intrathecal baclofen (pump), oral medications
School/work participation, functional goals
objective: GMFCS level (I–V): describe current functional mobility
Modified Ashworth Scale per muscle group (0=no increase to 4=rigid)
Tardieu Scale: quality of muscle reaction (X1–3) and angle of catch (R1, R2)
ROM: hip flexion/extension/abduction/internal rotation, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion
Hip migration percentage (if recent X-ray available)
Selective motor control: ability to isolate movement
Gait pattern observation: equinus, crouch, scissoring, stiff-knee, flexed trunk
Scoliosis: trunk deformity, Cobb angle if X-ray available
Contracture assessment: Thomas test (hip flexion), popliteal angle (hamstrings), Silfverskiöld (gastrocnemius vs soleus)
assessment_pearls: CP etiology: periventricular leukomalacia (premature), HIE (term), cerebral malformation, infection, stroke
CP is non-progressive — change in function suggests another diagnosis or secondary complications
Associated conditions: intellectual disability (50%), epilepsy (35%), visual impairment, communication disorder, pain, sleep problems
Hip surveillance: mandatory X-rays annually for all CP children — Reimer's migration index
Spasticity management ladder: oral → focal BoNT-A → intrathecal baclofen → orthopedic surgery
GMFCS predicts long-term mobility prognosis (stable after age 2)
Dyskinetic CP: often term birth, asphyxia/kernicterus, basal ganglia injury on MRI; relatively preserved cognition
Walking prognosis: sitting independently by 2yo = likely walker; not sitting by 4yo = unlikely independent ambulator
plan_template: Botulinum toxin-A (BoNT-A): focal muscle injection every 3–6 months; target spastic muscles (gastrocnemius, hamstrings, hip adductors, biceps)
Oral spasticity: baclofen 5–80mg/day (TID); tizanidine 4–36mg/day; diazepam (short-term)
Intrathecal baclofen (ITB): for severe generalized spasticity (GMFCS IV–V); pump implant
Selective dorsal rhizotomy (SDR): spastic diplegia, GMFCS II–III, good selective motor control
Orthotics: AFOs (hinged/fixed/dynamic), hand splints, KAFO — orthopedic review
Physiotherapy: stretch program, strength, gait training, mobility aids, aqua therapy
Occupational therapy: upper limb function, ADL training, assistive technology
Speech and language therapy: swallowing, communication, AAC systems
Hip surveillance X-ray: annually; refer to orthopedics if migration >30%
Seizures: AED per seizure type (levetiracetam often preferred in CP)
Multidisciplinary clinic: physiatry, pediatric neurology, orthopaedics, PT/OT/SLP, nutrition, social work

bedside_scales: GMFCS (I–V): I=walks without limitations, V=transported in manual wheelchair
MACS (I–V): I=handles objects easily, V=does not handle objects
Modified Ashworth Scale (MAS 0–4): 0=no increase, 1=slight catch, 2=marked increase, 3=considerable, 4=rigid
Tardieu Scale: quality of muscle reaction (X0=no resistance, X1=slight catch, X2=clear catch, X3=clonus), angle of catch (R1), passive ROM (R2)
WeeFIM/PEDI: functional independence measures for children
neurologic_exam: Tone assessment: velocity-dependent spasticity (Ashworth), quality and angle of catch (Tardieu)
Selective motor control: SCALE (Selective Control Assessment of the Lower Extremity)
Gait analysis: observational gait analysis or instrumented 3D gait analysis
Lower extremity ROM: Thomas test (hip flexion contracture), popliteal angle (hamstrings), Silfverskiöld test (differentiates gastrocnemius from soleus tightness), foot deformity assessment
Rotational profile: femoral anteversion (internal foot progression angle + Craig's test), tibial torsion (thigh-foot angle), foot alignment
Hip X-ray: Reimer's migration index = head beyond Perkin's line / head width × 100%
Upper extremity: shoulder abduction/ER, elbow extension, forearm supination, wrist/finger extension
Sensory: two-point discrimination (often impaired in hemiplegia)
Primitive reflexes: ATNR persistence indicates severity

categories.nameZh: 自律神經、疼痛及其他中樞神經

subjective: Pain characteristics: PQRST (provocation, quality, radiation, severity 0–10, temporal pattern)
Neuropathic pain features: burning, shooting, electric shock, allodynia, hyperalgesia
Central sensitization: widespread pain, fatigue, cognitive symptoms, sleep disturbance
Autonomic symptoms: orthostatic dizziness/syncope, sweating abnormalities, temperature dysregulation, flushing
Bowel/bladder dysfunction: neurogenic bladder (urgency/retention), incontinence, constipation
CRPS features: regional pain, allodynia, autonomic changes (skin color/temperature/sweating), edema, trophic changes
Intracranial hypertension: morning headache, worse lying down, visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus
Spinal cord features: weakness/sensory loss level, bowel/bladder urgency, saddle anesthesia
Medication history: analgesics, opioids, anticonvulsants, antidepressants, current pain management
Functional impact: work, activity limitation, mood, quality of life
objective: Orthostatic vital signs: BP and HR supine → sitting → standing at 1, 3 minutes
Skin: color asymmetry (livedo, pallor, rubor), temperature asymmetry (infrared thermometer), sweating
Edema: distribution, pitting vs. non-pitting, trophic changes (hair loss, nail changes)
Sensory level testing: pin, light touch, vibration, proprioception — dermatomal level
Allodynia: pain response to light touch (cotton wool) in painful regions
Hyperalgesia: increased pain to pinprick in affected areas
Motor level: myotomal testing, upper vs. lower motor neuron pattern (level of cord injury)
Reflexes: hyperreflexia + clonus (UMN, above cord lesion) vs. absent (LMN, at/below cord lesion)
Fundoscopy: papilledema grading (Frisen scale 0–5) for IIH
Bladder: post-void residual by ultrasound
assessment_pearls: Chronic pain classification: nociceptive (structural damage) vs. neuropathic (nerve injury) vs. nociplastic (central sensitization)
CRPS Budapest criteria: pain disproportionate to event, allodynia/hyperalgesia, autonomic/trophic signs, at least 1 positive symptom in 3 of 4 categories + 1 sign in 2 of 4 categories
IIH (G93.2): obese young women, BMI >30, daily pressure headache, papilledema, LP opening pressure >25cmH2O, normal CSF composition
Neurogenic bladder: detrusor overactivity (spastic — frequency/urgency) vs. areflexia (flaccid — retention) — urodynamics defines type
POTS: HR rise ≥30 bpm (≥40 in <19yo) within 10 min of standing without orthostatic hypotension (≥30/15mmHg drop)
Orthostatic hypotension: ≥20mmHg systolic or ≥10mmHg diastolic drop within 3 min of standing
Syringomyelia: cape-like dissociated sensory loss (pain/temp lost, vibration/proprioception preserved), segmental weakness, hyporeflexia at level
plan_template: Neuropathic pain first-line: duloxetine 30–60mg/day, pregabalin 75–300mg BID, amitriptyline 10–75mg QHS, gabapentin 300–1200mg TID
Neuropathic pain topical: lidocaine patch, capsaicin 8% patch (CRPS/localized neuropathic pain)
Central sensitization/fibromyalgia: duloxetine + graded exercise + CBT + sleep hygiene
CRPS: aggressive early mobilization, pain team referral; consider sympathetic nerve block for refractory cases
IIH: acetazolamide 500–2000mg/day titrated; weight loss (7–10% target); LP as bridge if vision threatened; optic nerve sheath fenestration or shunt for severe vision loss
Neurogenic bladder (overactive): oxybutynin 5–10mg BID-TID or solifenacin 5–10mg QD; CIC if inadequate emptying
Neurogenic bladder (underactive): clean intermittent catheterization (CIC); urology referral
POTS: increase fluid/salt intake, compression stockings, tilt training, beta-blockers (propranolol), ivabradine, fludrocortisone
Orthostatic hypotension: fludrocortisone 0.1–0.3mg/day, midodrine 2.5–10mg TID
Pain psychology: CBT, ACT, multidisciplinary pain clinic referral for complex chronic pain

bedside_scales: NRS (Numeric Rating Scale 0–10) or VAS for pain severity
Brief Pain Inventory (BPI): severity and interference subscales
LANSS (Leeds Assessment of Neuropathic Symptoms and Signs ≥12/24 = neuropathic)
DN4 questionnaire: ≥4/10 = neuropathic pain
Fibromyalgia: ACR 2010 criteria — Widespread Pain Index (WPI 0–19) + Symptom Severity Scale (SSS 0–12); WPI≥7+SSS≥5 OR WPI3-6+SSS≥9
ASIA Impairment Scale (A=complete to E=normal) for spinal cord injury
Tilt table test: POTS diagnosis protocol
neurologic_exam: Allodynia: light touch with cotton wool or light brush in painful area — pain = allodynia (touch-evoked pain)
Hyperalgesia: pinprick (28g monofilament) — enhanced pain response
Sensory level: systematic dermatomal testing C2→S5 for pin, light touch, vibration (128Hz tuning fork), proprioception
Motor level: key muscles ASIA motor scoring (C5-T1, L2-S1)
Orthostatic vital signs protocol: 3 readings at 1min and 3min standing
Infrared thermometer: skin temperature comparison bilateral (>1°C asymmetry = CRPS)
Pupil examination: Horner syndrome (ptosis+miosis), tonic pupil (Adie's — autonomic dysfunction)
Heart rate variability: deep breathing test (6 breaths/min) — E:I ratio <1.2 suggests autonomic neuropathy

categories.nameZh: 發炎性及晶體性關節炎

subjective: Joint pattern: number (mono/oligo/poly), specific joints (small vs. large), symmetric vs. asymmetric
Morning stiffness: duration in minutes (>60 min = inflammatory)
Gout: acute severe pain, night onset, first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra), prior attacks, tophi
Dietary triggers: purines (organ meat, shellfish, red meat), alcohol (especially beer), fructose
Medications: diuretics, ASA, cyclosporine (raise uric acid); allopurinol/febuxostat (current dose)
RA features: symmetrical small joints (MCP, PIP, wrist), prolonged morning stiffness, fatigue, constitutional symptoms
Extra-articular: iritis/uveitis, skin nodules (RA), psoriasis/nail changes (psoriatic arthritis)
Reactive arthritis trigger: recent GI infection (Salmonella, Shigella) or STI (Chlamydia)
Family history: gout, RA, ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27 related)
Current DMARDs, biologics, NSAIDs, steroid use
objective: Joint exam: swelling (effusion vs. synovial thickening), warmth, erythema, tenderness (palpation)
Active vs. passive ROM limitation
Small joint involvement: MCP (subluxation, ulnar deviation in RA), PIP/DIP pattern
Wrist: synovitis, grip strength (dynamometer)
Knee: effusion (bulge sign, patellar tap), warmth, medial/lateral joint line tenderness
First MTP: erythema, swelling, extreme tenderness (podagra)
Tophi: ear helix, olecranon bursa, Achilles tendon, first MTP, distal phalanges
Skin: psoriatic plaques (extensor surfaces, scalp, umbilicus, natal cleft), nail pitting/onycholysis
Eye: uveitis symptoms (red eye, pain, blurred vision) — slit lamp exam
Enthesitis sites: Achilles insertion, plantar fascia, patellar tendon, iliac crest
assessment_pearls: Gout: serum urate often normal DURING acute attack (redistributes into joint); synovial fluid crystals remain gold standard (MSU: needle-shaped, negatively birefringent under polarized light)
Pseudogout (CPPD): rhomboid/square crystals, weakly positively birefringent; chondrocalcinosis on X-ray (knee, wrist, symphysis pubis)
RA 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria: ≥6/10 points (joint count, serology, acute-phase reactants, symptom duration)
Anti-CCP: 95% specific for RA; RF 75–80% sensitive; both positive = worse prognosis (erosive disease)
Septic arthritis emergency: Staphylococcus most common; synovial WBC >50,000 (often >100,000), PMN>90%; requires urgent drainage + IV antibiotics
Reactive arthritis: "can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree" (iritis, urethritis, arthritis)
Psoriatic arthritis patterns: DIP involvement, dactylitis ("sausage digit"), asymmetric oligoarthritis, axial disease, arthritis mutilans
Target uric acid: <360 μmol/L (<6 mg/dL) standard; <300 μmol/L (<5 mg/dL) for tophaceous gout
plan_template: Acute gout attack: NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID × 5–7 days) OR colchicine (1.2mg then 0.6mg 1hr later, then 0.6mg BID × 5–7 days) OR prednisone 30–40mg/day × 3–5 days
Gout joint aspiration + intra-articular steroid (triamcinolone 40mg for large joint) if single joint
Start urate-lowering therapy (ULT): allopurinol starting 50–100mg/day, titrate slowly to target; check HLA-B*5801 in high-risk populations (Han Chinese)
Gout prophylaxis during ULT initiation: colchicine 0.6mg QD or BID for 3–6 months
Febuxostat 40–80mg/day if allopurinol intolerant or ineffective
RA initial: methotrexate 10–25mg/week + folic acid; add hydroxychloroquine/sulfasalazine (combination therapy)
RA target: DAS28 <2.6 (remission) or <3.2 (low activity) — treat to target strategy
RA biologics: TNF inhibitor (etanercept, adalimumab) if inadequate MTX response; IL-6 inhibitor or JAK inhibitor alternatives
Psoriatic arthritis: NSAIDs for peripheral joint; MTX for skin predominant; TNF/IL-17 inhibitors for moderate-severe
Septic arthritis: aspiration + culture-guided IV antibiotics (cefazolin for MSSA, vancomycin for MRSA/empiric) + daily joint aspiration or surgical drainage
