bedside_scales: DAS28 (Disease Activity Score 28): swollen + tender joint count, ESR or CRP, patient global VAS; <2.6=remission, <3.2=low activity, 3.2-5.1=moderate, >5.1=high
CDAI (Clinical Disease Activity Index): swollen + tender joints + patient global + physician global; no lab needed
HAQ-DI (Health Assessment Questionnaire): functional disability 0–3
ACR20/50/70 response criteria: 20/50/70% improvement in tender + swollen joints + 3 of 5 other criteria
neurologic_exam: Joint aspiration technique and fluid interpretation: WBC<2000=non-inflammatory, 2000-50000=inflammatory, >50000=septic
Synovial fluid crystal microscopy: MSU (gout: needle, negatively birefringent), CPPD (rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent)
X-ray RA findings: marginal erosions at MCP/wrist/feet, periarticular osteopenia, joint space narrowing
X-ray gout: "rat bite" erosions with overhanging edge, tophi
Grip strength (dynamometer): objective measure of hand disease
Schirmer's test for dry eyes: <5mm in 5min = positive (secondary Sjögren's in RA)
Enthesitis examination: tender at Achilles insertion, plantar fascia origin, patellar tendon
Dactylitis: "sausage digit" — diffuse swelling entire digit including flexor tendon sheath

subjective: SLE: butterfly rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopecia, serositis (pleuritis/pericarditis), renal involvement, hematologic, neuropsychiatric symptoms
Scleroderma/SSc: Raynaud's phenomenon (triphasic: white→blue→red), skin thickening, dysphagia/GERD, ILD symptoms, pulmonary hypertension
Limited vs. diffuse SSc: skin distribution (CREST: calcinosis, Raynaud, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, telangiectasia)
Sjogren's: dry eyes (sicca — sandy/gritty), dry mouth, joint pain, fatigue, neuropathy
Inflammatory myopathy (PM/DM): proximal muscle weakness (difficulty rising from chair, lifting arms), skin changes
Vasculitis pattern: headache/jaw claudication/visual loss (GCA), sinusitis/hemoptysis (GPA), palpable purpura (small vessel)
Constitutional: fever, weight loss, fatigue, night sweats (most CTDs)
Drug history: DMARDs, biologics, steroids; hydralazine/procainamide/minocycline (drug-induced lupus)
objective: Skin: malar rash (cheeks/nose bridge, spares nasolabial folds — SLE), discoid lesions, photosensitivity distribution
Heliotrope rash (periorbital violaceous edema — DM), Gottron's papules (over MCP/PIP knuckles)
Skin thickening: Modified Rodnan Skin Score (mRSS) 0–51 for SSc
Raynaud's: digital color changes, digital pits, ulcers, calcinosis, sclerodactyly
Nailfold capillaroscopy: dilated/tortuous loops, hemorrhages, avascular areas
Proximal muscle strength: deltoids (abduction 5/5?), hip flexors (stand from chair?)
Lung: bibasal Velcro crackles (ILD), reduced chest expansion (AS)
Heart: pericardial rub, S3 (myocarditis, effusion)
Renal: blood pressure, edema, urinalysis (RBC casts = active glomerulonephritis, proteinuria)
Eye: Schirmer's test, anterior uveitis signs (GCA → fundoscopy for AION)
assessment_pearls: SLE classification: SLICC 2012 (≥4 criteria, ≥1 clinical + ≥1 immunological) or ACR/EULAR 2019 (≥10 points)

Antibody profile: ANA (sensitive but not specific), anti-dsDNA (SLE activity + nephritis), anti-Sm (SLE specific), anti-Ro/SSA+La/SSB (Sjogren's, neonatal lupus), anti-Scl-70 (diffuse SSc), anti-centromere (limited SSc/CREST), anti-Jo1 (antisynthetase syndrome)
Lupus nephritis biopsy: ISN/RPS Class I–VI; Class III (focal) + IV (diffuse) = aggressive treatment with mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide
SSc-ILD: HRCT chest (ground glass opacities, honeycombing) + PFTs (restrictive) + 6MWT; nintedanib for SSc-ILD
Anti-MDA5 DM: amyopathic, rapidly progressive ILD, skin ulcers — very high mortality risk
GCA: ESR>50 + CRP, temporal artery tenderness/nodularity/absent pulse; temporal artery biopsy (gold standard); start prednisone IMMEDIATELY if vision threatened
ANCA vasculitis: PR3-ANCA (GPA) vs MPO-ANCA (MPA); biopsy for definitive diagnosis
plan_template: SLE: hydroxychloroquine 5mg/kg/day (all patients); prednisone for flares (taper to <7.5mg/day); MTX/azathioprine organ-sparing; mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide for lupus nephritis
Belimumab (anti-BLyS) or anifrolumab (anti-IFN receptor): for inadequate SLE control on standard therapy
Lupus nephritis III/IV: high-dose prednisone + mycophenolate (EURO-LUPUS regimen) OR cyclophosphamide; maintenance mycophenolate/azathioprine
SSc Raynaud/digital ulcers: CCBs (nifedipine), PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil), prostacyclin infusion (iloprost) for severe
SSc GERD: high-dose PPI + prokinetics
SSc-PAH: endothelin receptor antagonists (bosentan/macitentan), PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil), prostacyclins; specialist referral
SSc-ILD: nintedanib (pirfenidone alternative); mycophenolate for immunosuppression
PM/DM: prednisone 1mg/kg/day × 4–8 weeks then taper; add MTX or azathioprine for steroid-sparing; IVIG for refractory
GCA: prednisone 60mg/day (1mg/kg if vision loss) immediately; tocilizumab for steroid-sparing; taper over 12–24 months
ANCA vasculitis: rituximab or cyclophosphamide + high-dose prednisone for induction; rituximab maintenance

bedside_scales: SLEDAI-2K: ≥1=active, ≥6=moderate activity, ≥12=high activity
SLICC/ACR Damage Index (SDI 0–47): cumulative irreversible damage in SLE
Modified Rodnan Skin Score (mRSS 0–51): 17 body areas scored 0–3 for SSc
MMT-8 (Manual Muscle Testing-8): for inflammatory myopathy (deltoids, biceps, wrist extensors, quadriceps, hip flexors, hip abductors)
Physician Global Assessment (PGA) 0–10: overall disease activity
neurologic_exam: Proximal muscle testing: standard 5-point scale — deltoids (arm at shoulder level), hip flexors (from lying: lift leg straight), hip flexors from sitting (stand without arms)
Gottron's sign: erythematous/violaceous macules on knuckle pads (fingers, elbows, knees) — DM
Nailfold capillaroscopy: abnormal patterns in SSc, DM, MCTD (dilated capillaries, avascular areas)
CNS lupus: seizures, psychosis, chorea, myelopathy, cranial/peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline
Mononeuritis multiplex in vasculitis: asymmetric involvement of multiple named peripheral nerves
Sicca assessment: Schirmer's test <5mm/5min = abnormal; unstimulated whole saliva <1.5ml/15min = abnormal
Pulmonary: Velcro crackles (ILD bases), auscultation of P2 (PAH — loud), pleural rub (serositis)

subjective: Osteoporosis: prior low-energy fractures, vertebral fracture (loss of height ≥2cm, kyphosis), hip fracture history
Fracture risk factors: age, sex (post-menopausal female), family history of hip fracture, smoking, alcohol >3U/day, steroid use, low BMI (<18.5), falls, chronic disease
FRAX score (10-year probability) if available
Calcium intake (dietary + supplements) and Vitamin D history
Bisphosphonate history: duration, dental procedures (osteonecrosis of jaw risk), atypical thigh/groin pain
Paget's disease: bone pain, deformity (bowing of limbs, skull enlargement), hearing loss
Avascular necrosis risk: steroids (most common), alcohol, sickle cell, decompression illness, trauma, radiation
Joint deformity: hallux valgus (bunion), hammer toes, claw toes, boutonnière/swan-neck finger deformity
Knee internal derangement: mechanism of injury, locking, giving way, instability, effusion
Patellofemoral pain syndrome: anterior knee pain with stairs, prolonged sitting ("movie sign"), squatting
objective: Height measurement: compare to historical (vertebral compression → height loss)
Kyphotic posture, protuberant abdomen (multiple vertebral compression fractures)
Gait: antalgic, Trendelenburg, shortened stride (hip/knee pain)
Balance: Romberg, single-leg stand, tandem walk (falls risk assessment)
DEXA results: BMD T-score (lumbar spine L1–L4, total hip, femoral neck)
Hallux valgus angle, bunion prominence, lesser toe deformities
Knee: effusion (patella ballottement, bulge sign), joint line tenderness (medial/lateral), ROM, crepitus
Knee ligament tests: Lachman (ACL), anterior/posterior drawer, valgus/varus stress (MCL/LCL)
Meniscal tests: McMurray (pain/click with flexion-rotation), Thessaly test (weight-bearing rotation)
Paget's: bowing of tibia/femur, skull enlargement, local warmth, elevated ALP
Avascular necrosis: pain on axial loading (trochanteric/groin), decreased ROM hip
assessment_pearls: Osteoporosis diagnosis: DEXA T-score ≤-2.5 at lumbar spine or proximal femur; osteopenia -1.0 to -2.5; exclude secondary causes (thyroid, PTH, celiac, malabsorption, medications)
Treatment threshold (NOF guidelines): T-score ≤-2.5 OR prior hip/vertebral fracture OR FRAX ≥3% (hip) or ≥20% (major fracture)
Bisphosphonate holiday: consider after 5 years oral / 3 years IV zoledronate; retest DEXA/FRAX; restart if high risk
Atypical femur fracture (AFF): subtrochanteric/diaphyseal, transverse, periosteal thickening, prodromal thigh pain, bilateral in 28%; stop bisphosphonate
MRONJ (medication-related osteonecrosis of jaw): risk with BP/denosumab + invasive dental procedures — inform all patients, consider drug holiday before elective procedures
Paget's disease: alkaline phosphatase markedly elevated (often >10× upper normal), normal calcium; bone scan for extent; radiograph lytic → mixed → sclerotic phases
Avascular necrosis staging: Ficat/Steinberg 0–IV; MRI most sensitive (marrow edema stage I); collapse = stage III
Knee OA with varus deformity: medial compartment disease — unloader brace if not surgical candidate
plan_template: Osteoporosis pharmacotherapy: bisphosphonates first-line (alendronate 70mg weekly PO, or zoledronate 5mg IV annually); denosumab 60mg SC 6-monthly if bisphosphonate intolerant or low GFR
Teriparatide or abaloparatide: anabolic for high fracture risk (severe osteoporosis, multiple fractures, T-score <-3.5)
Romosozumab 210mg SC monthly × 12 months: dual anabolic+anti-resorptive; transition to anti-resorptive after
Calcium 1000–1200mg/day + Vitamin D 800–2000 IU/day (all osteoporosis patients)
Falls prevention: balance/resistance training, vision correction, medication review, home hazard removal, hip protectors
Fracture liaison service (FLS) referral after any fragility fracture
Paget's disease: zoledronate 5mg IV single infusion (first-line); monitor ALP every 6–12 months
Avascular necrosis early stage: unloading (partial weight-bearing), core decompression + bone grafting
Avascular necrosis late stage (collapse): total joint replacement (TJR)
Hallux valgus: wide/low-heeled shoes, orthotics; bunionectomy (osteotomy) if failed conservative × 3–6 months
ACL injury: conservative + PT for low-demand patients; surgical reconstruction for young/active patients with instability
Knee OA: physiotherapy, weight loss, NSAIDs; intra-articular steroid/hyaluronic acid; UKA or TKA for end-stage

bedside_scales: DEXA T-score: >-1.0=normal, -1.0 to -2.5=osteopenia, ≤-2.5=osteoporosis
FRAX: 10-year probability of major fracture and hip fracture (www.sheffield.ac.uk/FRAX)
Timed Up and Go (TUG): >12s = fall risk; >20s = significantly impaired; <10s = normal
Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB 0–12): balance, gait speed, chair stand
WOMAC (0–100): OA pain, stiffness, function; higher = worse
KOOS (0–100): pain, symptoms, function, quality of life subscales; lower = worse
neurologic_exam: Knee ligament exam: Lachman (ACL — most sensitive), anterior/posterior drawer, valgus/varus stress tests at 0° and 30°
Meniscal tests: McMurray (flex+rotate), Thessaly (standing weight-bearing rotation at 20° flexion), Apley compression/distraction
Patellar tests: patellar ballottement (effusion), patellar grind/compression test (chondromalacia), apprehension test (subluxation)
Hip tests: FABER/Patrick (hip/SI joint), FADIR/impingement test, Thomas test (flexion contracture), Trendelenburg (abductor weakness)
Foot/ankle: subtalar ROM, first MTP dorsiflexion, Coleman block test (heel varus), Thompson test (Achilles integrity)
Single-leg squat: assess dynamic knee valgus, Trendelenburg, core stability
Leg length discrepancy: real (ASIS to medial malleolus) vs. apparent (umbilicus to medial malleolus)
